narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid LSAT score and upper-middle family income background. However, the admissions model predicted a 91.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic performance in school, specifically your Year 1 and Year 3 grades, which placed you in the lowest decile compared to your peers. These early and later low rankings had the strongest influence, pushing the model heavily toward expecting bar failure rather than success. Your LSAT score, slightly above average, helped counter this by nudging the prediction toward passing, showing your potential in standardized testing. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA, just below the typical 3.21 average, offered a small positive pull toward success, while your upper-middle family income slightly favored failure in the model's eyes, perhaps reflecting patterns in the data. Being a full-time student was a minor positive factor, aligning with the vast majority who pursue law this way. Overall, your grades in the bottom decile for both early and later years stood out as far weaker than typical students who pass the bar, overshadowing your other solid attributes. While this is disappointing, especially given the challenges many Black women face in academic pipelines, it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, focus on improving your grades to climb those school deciles, perhaps through tutoring or additional coursework, and consider retaking the LSAT if possible to boost it further. You might also explore part-time programs or schools with strong support for underrepresented students like yourself. Keep pushing forward—many have turned similar setbacks into bar-passing triumphs. We're rooting for you.
